theorem_title: Lean Eval: complementary polynomial on the unit circle
theorem_statement: Let P be a complex polynomial. If |P(z)| <= 1 for every point z on the unit circle, then there exists a complex polynomial Q with the same natural degree as P such that |P(z)|^2 + |Q(z)|^2 = 1 for every z on the unit circle.
raw_proof_text:
The key tool is the Fejer-Riesz lemma. If T(e^{it}) = sum_{k=-n}^n c_k e^{ikt} is a nonnegative trigonometric polynomial, then there is a complex polynomial Q(z) = q_0 + q_1 z + ... + q_n z^n such that T(e^{it}) = |Q(e^{it})|^2 for all t.

Sketch of Fejer-Riesz: write z = e^{it}. Multiplying the trigonometric polynomial by z^n gives an ordinary complex polynomial R(z) = z^n T(z) of degree at most 2n. Since T is nonnegative on the unit circle, zeros of R on the unit circle have even multiplicity. The symmetry of T implies that if alpha != 0 is a zero of R, then 1 / conjugate(alpha) is also a zero with the same multiplicity. Choose one zero from each reciprocal pair off the unit circle and half of each even unit-circle zero. This produces a polynomial Q of degree at most n such that R(z) = Q(z) Q_sharp(z), where Q_sharp(z) = z^n conjugate(Q(1 / conjugate z)). On the unit circle, Q_sharp(z) = z^n conjugate(Q(z)), so R(z) = z^n |Q(z)|^2. Since R(z) = z^n T(z), we get T(z) = |Q(z)|^2 on the unit circle.

Apply Fejer-Riesz to T(z) = 1 - |P(z)|^2 on the unit circle. The hypothesis |P(z)| <= 1 implies T(z) >= 0 on the unit circle. Since P has degree at most n, the function |P(e^{it})|^2 is a trigonometric polynomial with frequencies between -n and n: if P(z) = sum_{k=0}^n a_k z^k, then on the unit circle, |P(z)|^2 = P(z) conjugate(P(z)) = (sum_k a_k z^k)(sum_l conjugate(a_l) z^{-l}) = sum_{k,l} a_k conjugate(a_l) z^{k-l}. Thus T is also a nonnegative trigonometric polynomial with frequencies between -n and n.

By Fejer-Riesz, there exists Q of degree at most n such that T(z) = |Q(z)|^2 on the unit circle. Therefore 1 - |P(z)|^2 = |Q(z)|^2, equivalently |P(z)|^2 + |Q(z)|^2 = 1 for every point z on the unit circle.

Likely Formal Islands graph: show 1 - |P|^2 is a nonnegative trigonometric polynomial; invoke or prove Fejer-Riesz; extract Q; verify the unit-circle identity; handle the degree condition. The most likely formal island is the trigonometric-polynomial algebra for |P(z)|^2 and the final identity after assuming Fejer-Riesz. The full Fejer-Riesz factorization is likely remaining burden.
